reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Merit-based selection and subsidiarity: conservative values endorse selection by demonstrated ability rather than socioeconomic status; however, the mechanism must be credible and transparent. Parental choice and school autonomy matter: non-state maintainers must retain freedom to set their own admission rules.
reactions.interest: Parents who value merit-based advancement for their children; maintainers (especially church and foundation schools) asserting institutional autonomy; families who regard exam-based selection as objective and fair.
reactions.public_good_frame: A selection system that recognizes and develops talent wherever it appears, without regard to family wealth, serves both individual flourishing and social productivity. Transparency and objectivity in admission protect equal dignity and trust in institutions.
reactions.argument: We support the aim of reducing SES skew in selective admission — a merit-based system ought to select ability, not purchasing power. However, this scenario rests on three unverified assumptions that make it conditional: (1) a genuinely less-coachable assessment can be designed and validated; (2) a usable SES proxy exists that resists gaming at application; (3) non-state maintainers retain the autonomy to opt out if they judge the reformed rules inconsistent with their mission. The scenario itself documents that no such SES register exists in Hungary and any proxy would be gameable. Absent genuine technical solutions, the reform risks relabelling SES sorting as 'merit' — which is worse than transparent explicit selection because it claims false objectivity. If the assessment is still coachable, affluent parents will simply coach differently, and the reform becomes a cost-transfer to lower-SES families who must now master a new, opaque instrument. We oppose any reform that claims to measure merit while actually measuring test-preparation resources.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S1 if: (a) independent psychometric evidence shows the proposed assessment is materially more resistant to coaching than the current exam, with pilot data on coaching-sensitivity and SES gradients; (b) any SES proxy is demonstrated on administrative data from a comparable system to survive gaming and not simply shift advantage to families who understand the proxy; (c) non-state maintainers are explicitly exempted unless they volunteer, so that institutional autonomy is preserved; (d) the outreach programme is pilot-tested and shows genuine uptake elasticity by SES/ethnicity before rollout. We would oppose if the reform is either relabelled sorting without genuine technical change, or if it imposes SES-conscious quotas on maintainers against their consent.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Merit-based development of talent; parental choice and school autonomy; subsidiarity — decisions should reside with families and maintainers, not central planning. Conservative values reject centrally-mandated caps on access to selective education.
reactions.interest: Parents and families who value selective education as a vehicle for talent development; maintainers (especially church and foundation schools) who regard early selection as consistent with their institutional mission; students of high ability regardless of SES who benefit from peer composition and accelerated curriculum.
reactions.public_good_frame: Allowing families to choose selective education and enabling schools to select by merit serves both individual flourishing and efficient talent development. Early identification of high ability and peer grouping by ability supports pedagogical effectiveness and reduces waste of potential. Competition between maintainers (state and non-state) ensures responsiveness to parental preference.
reactions.argument: We oppose S2 because it uses central capping to restrict parental choice and institutional autonomy — the core mechanism is coercive allocation by the state, not consensual choice. The scenario's own evidence identifies the fatal flaw: capping state-maintained selective seats will be offset by non-state maintainers absorbing displaced demand. This is not a bug; it is a feature of a pluralist system. If you cap state schools, families with resources simply move to church and foundation schools — you do not reduce early selection, you privatise it and weaken the state sector's ability to serve high-ability low-SES pupils who cannot access private alternatives. The scenario documents this risk as 'near-certain' yet proposes it anyway. A decade-long phase-down also entrenches bureaucratic complexity (parallel regimes, grandfathered cohorts, constant monitoring) while delivering no net reduction in selection. The peer-composition benefit is contested and depends on non-state offset not occurring — precisely what the scenario predicts will occur. We prefer open competition: if a family values selective education, they should have access to it; if a school believes in early selection, it should be free to practise it; if a region wants a comprehensive system, it should be free to choose it. Mandated shrinkage from the centre violates subsidiarity and will fail through the non-state offset mechanism.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S2 only if: (a) non-state maintainers can be compelled by legislation to respect the same intake caps as state schools — a condition the scenario identifies as 'legally and politically contested', which means it is implausible; (b) OR if the reform is reframed as voluntary per-district decision-making, where a locality can choose to phase down selective intake consensually, rather than a national cap imposed on mixed-maintainer systems. In the absence of either, S2 is coercive central planning that will fail by offset and damage the state sector without reducing overall selection. We oppose it.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Merit-based tracking and ability grouping as pedagogically sound; parental choice and school autonomy; subsidiarity; tradition of strong specialist pedagogy in selective schools. Conservative values are skeptical of 'one-size-fits-all' comprehensive structures that deny parental choice and school differentiation.
reactions.interest: Parents and families who value early identification and tracking by ability as serving their children's learning outcomes; maintainers (especially church and foundation schools) asserting the right to selective education; specialist teachers and subject-matter experts in elite tracks; families who regard comprehensive schools as erosive of academic standards and peer-group autonomy.
reactions.public_good_frame: A system that identifies talent early, groups students by ability and pace, invests in specialized pedagogy, and allows parental choice among school types serves both learning outcomes and individual dignity. Forcing mixed-ability grouping throughout lower secondary delays advanced content for able pupils and can dilute outcomes. Competition among school types drives quality and responsiveness.
reactions.argument: We oppose S3 because it replaces parental choice and meritocratic differentiation with a centrally-mandated comprehensive structure that denies families the right to selective education and schools the autonomy to serve specialized cohorts. The scenario's own evidence reveals the fatal assumption: it assumes sorting does not migrate to non-state maintainers and school choice — precisely what the scenario identifies as 'the largest risk' and what the maintainer-plurality evidence predicts will occur. Hungary will not become Poland; it will become a two-tier system where state comprehensive schools warehouse general pupils while church, foundation, and eventually expanded private schools absorb selective demand. The equity gain the scenario predicts depends on this non-migration not happening; if it does happen (the base-case prediction), you have merely relocated sorting from transparent, state-managed selection to opaque school choice between general schools and non-state maintainers — arguably a worse outcome for transparency and equal dignity. The peer-effect mechanism is contested; the within-decade equity gains are asserted, not evidenced for Hungary; the teacher supply assumption contradicts the input's own strong finding of severe shortage; and the political durability is weak — the scenario documents that no durable window exists absent cross-party constitutional insulation, which is unrealistic in Hungary. We also note that S3 is the costliest scenario and requires sustained retraining, early-support infrastructure, and teacher stabilization that makes it highly vulnerable to the budget-cycle risk that compromises S4. Abolishing early selection without containing non-state offset and without realistic teacher supply and political durability is ideological central planning, not evidence-driven reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support a structural delay of selection to age 14-15 only if: (a) non-state maintainers are compelled by law to respect the comprehensive structure or to operate only at post-14 level — a condition we regard as politically implausible and subsidiary-violating; (b) OR the reform is voluntary per-district, allowing localities that choose comprehensiveness to opt in, while preserving choice for others; (c) AND pilot data from at least two Hungarian districts show that sorting does not migrate significantly to school choice and non-state maintainers, with 5-year outcome tracking; (d) AND teacher supply and retraining data demonstrate that a stabilized, adequately trained teaching workforce can be assembled for comprehensive upper grades without degrading state-school quality. In the absence of these conditions, S3 is ideological rather than evidence-based, and we oppose it on grounds of both subsidiarity and realistic assessment of political durability and implementation risk.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Merit-based selection preserved; school autonomy and parental choice intact; subsidiarity — decisions on resource allocation remain with families and schools, not central mandates. Conservative values support investment in quality and standards, not structural disruption.
reactions.interest: Parents and families who value merit-based selection and early tracking; maintainers who regard selective education as part of their institutional mission; affluent families who benefit from selective schools and prefer non-disruptive reform; fiscal conservatives who prefer lower-cost, lower-disruption policy.
reactions.public_good_frame: Investing in quality teaching, standards, and targeted support in disadvantaged schools serves both excellence and opportunity. Merit-based selection identifies talent; quality investment develops it across the system. This approach respects institutional autonomy, parental choice, and family preference while pursuing equity through strength rather than through coercive restructuring.
reactions.argument: S4 is the most honest of the four scenarios because it does not promise to solve early-selection effects through administrative rearrangement — it explicitly trades structural change for compensatory investment. We support this trade-off in principle. However, the scenario's own evidence reveals the critical vulnerability: targeted equity funding is 'the first cut in fiscal consolidation', and Hungarian budget cycles are hostile to sustained multi-year commitment outside entitlements. Portuguese TEIP worked in a different fiscal regime and at a different selection age (13-14 vs 10-12); transfer to Hungary is untested. The scenario also documents that teacher shortage is a 'strong binding constraint' — compensatory funding for disadvantaged schools fails if you cannot staff them, and Hungarian teacher supply is severely constrained. The core claim — that 'compensation can meaningfully offset, not just mask, the effects of early sorting' — is frankly unverifiable ex ante and contradicted by the fact that no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's age-10-12 selection age. S4 risks legitimising early selection permanently by appearing to address its effects downstream while actually only masking them. We support investment in general schools and teaching quality as intrinsically valuable, but we do not accept the framing that this substitutes for addressing selection itself.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S4 conditionally if: (a) the targeted funding is genuinely ring-fenced in a multi-year budget agreement (ideally 7-10 years) with exemption from fiscal consolidation cuts — this requires either legislative super-majority or structural earmarking that Hungary currently lacks; (b) teacher supply for disadvantaged schools is demonstrably stabilized through recruitment incentives or retraining, with baseline and target workforce data; (c) the scenario is explicitly framed as compensation for, not substitution for, the effects of early selection — i.e., the government does not claim S4 'solves' equity, only mitigates downstream harm; (d) pilot evaluation in 2-3 disadvantaged-school networks shows genuine outcome improvement (not merely reduced inequality of decline) within 5 years, with comparison groups. We would oppose S4 if it is presented as a complete solution to selection effects, if the funding is politically unprotected from budget cycles, or if teacher supply cannot be stabilized. In that case, it becomes a costly symbolic gesture that entrenches selection while underfunding the compensatory investment.
